question:
Let $p(z)$ be a polynomial of degree $n$ all of whose zeros have absolute
value 1 in the complex  plane. Put $g(z) = p(z)/z^{n/2}$. Show that all zeros
of $g'(z) = 0$ have absolute value 1.
solution:
Note that it is implicit in the problem that $p$ is nonconstant,
one may take any branch of the square root, and that $z=0$ should be
ignored.

\textbf{First solution:}
Write $p(z) = c \prod_{j=1}^n (z-r_j)$, so that
\[
\frac{g'(z)}{g(z)} = \frac{1}{2z} \sum_{j=1}^n \frac{z+r_j}{z-r_j}.
\]
Now if $z\neq r_j$ for all $j$,then
\[
\frac{z+r_j}{z-r_j}
= \frac{(z+r_j)(\overline{z}-\overline{r_j})}{|z-r_j|^2}
= \frac{|z|^2-1+2 \mathrm{Im}(\overline{z} r_j)}{|z-r_j|^2},
\]
and so
\[
\mathrm{Re}\, \frac{z g'(z)}{g(z)} = \frac{|z|^2-1}{2} \left(
\sum_j \frac{1}{|z-r_j|^2} \right).
\]
Since the quantity in parentheses
is positive, $g'(z)/g(z)$ can be $0$ only if $|z|=1$. If on the other
hand $z = r_j$ for some $j$, then $|z|=1$ anyway.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Write $p(z) = c\prod_{j=1}^n (z-r_j)$, so that
\[
\frac{g'(z)}{g(z)}
= \sum_{j=1}^n \left( \frac{1}{z-r_j} - \frac{1}{2z} \right).
\]
We first check that $g'(z) \neq 0$ whenever $z$ is real and $z>1$.
In this case, for $r_j = e^{i \theta_j}$, we have $z - r_j =
(z - \cos (\theta_j)) + \sin(\theta_j) i$, so the real part of
$\frac{1}{z-r_j} - \frac{1}{2z}$ is
\[
\frac{z - \cos(\theta_j)}{z^2 - 2z \cos(\theta_j) + 1} - \frac{1}{2z}
= \frac{z^2-1}{2z(z^2 - 2z \cos(\theta_j) + 1)} > 0.
\]
Hence $g'(z)/g(z)$ has positive real part, so $g'(z)/g(z)$ and hence
$g(z)$ are nonzero.

Applying the same argument after replacing $p(z)$ by $p(e^{i \theta} z)$,
we deduce that $g'$ cannot have any roots outside the unit circle.
Applying the same argument after replacing $p(z)$ by $z^n p(1/z)$, we
also deduce that $g'$ cannot have any roots inside the unit circle.
Hence all roots of $g'$ have absolute value 1, as desired.

\textbf{Third solution:}
Write $p(z) = c \prod_{j=1}^n (z - r_j)$ and
put $r_j = e^{2 i \theta_j}$. Note that $g(e^{2 i \theta})$
is equal to a nonzero constant times
\begin{align*}
h(\theta) &= \prod_{j=1}^n \frac{e^{i (\theta + \theta_j)}
- e^{-i(\theta + \theta_j)}}{2i} = \prod_{j=1}^n \sin(\theta +\theta_j).
\end{align*}
Since $h$ has at least $2n$ roots (counting multiplicity)
in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$, $h'$ does also by repeated application of
Rolle's theorem. Since $g'(e^{2 i \theta}) = 2i e^{2i \theta} h'(\theta)$,
$g'(z^2)$ has at least $2n$ roots on the unit circle. Since $g'(z^2)$ is equal to
$z^{-n-1}$ times a polynomial of degree $2n$, $g'(z^2)$ has all roots on the
unit circle, as then does $g'(z)$.

\textbf{Remarks:}
The second solution imitates the proof of the Gauss-Lucas theorem:
the roots of the derivative of a complex polynomial lie in the convex hull of
the roots of the original polynomial.
The second solution is close to problem B3 from the 2000 Putnam.
A hybrid between the first and third solutions is to check that
on the unit circle, $\mathrm{Re}(zg'(z)/g(z)) = 0$ while between any
two roots of $p$, $\mathrm{Im}(zg'(z)/g(z))$ runs from $+\infty$ to $-\infty$
and so must have a zero crossing. (This only works when $p$ has distinct roots,
but the general case follows by the continuity of the roots of a polynomial
as functions of the coefficients.)
One can also construct a  solution using Rouch\'e's theorem.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $polynomial(complexvar)$ be a polynomial of degree $degreenum$ all of whose zeros have absolute
value 1 in the complex  plane. Put $quotientfun(complexvar) = polynomial(complexvar)/complexvar^{degreenum/2}$. Show that all zeros
of $quotientfun'(complexvar) = 0$ have absolute value 1.
descriptive_long.solution:
Note that it is implicit in the problem that $polynomial$ is nonconstant,
one may take any branch of the square root, and that $complexvar=0$ should be
ignored.

\textbf{First solution:}
Write $polynomial(complexvar) = leadingconst \prod_{indexvar=1}^{degreenum} (complexvar-unitroot)$, so that
\[
\frac{quotientfun'(complexvar)}{quotientfun(complexvar)} = \frac{1}{2complexvar} \sum_{indexvar=1}^{degreenum} \frac{complexvar+unitroot}{complexvar-unitroot}.
\]
Now if $complexvar\neq unitroot$ for all $indexvar$,then
\[
\frac{complexvar+unitroot}{complexvar-unitroot}
= \frac{(complexvar+unitroot)(\overline{complexvar}-\overline{unitroot})}{|complexvar-unitroot|^2}
= \frac{|complexvar|^2-1+2 \mathrm{Im}(\overline{complexvar} unitroot)}{|complexvar-unitroot|^2},
\]
and so
\[
\mathrm{Re}\, \frac{complexvar \!\, quotientfun'(complexvar)}{quotientfun(complexvar)} = \frac{|complexvar|^2-1}{2} \left(
\sum_{indexvar} \frac{1}{|complexvar-unitroot|^2} \right).
\]
Since the quantity in parentheses
is positive, $quotientfun'(complexvar)/quotientfun(complexvar)$ can be $0$ only if $|complexvar|=1$. If on the other
hand $complexvar = unitroot$ for some $indexvar$, then $|complexvar|=1$ anyway.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Write $polynomial(complexvar) = leadingconst\prod_{indexvar=1}^{degreenum} (complexvar-unitroot)$, so that
\[
\frac{quotientfun'(complexvar)}{quotientfun(complexvar)}
= \sum_{indexvar=1}^{degreenum} \left( \frac{1}{complexvar-unitroot} - \frac{1}{2complexvar} \right).
\]
We first check that $quotientfun'(complexvar) \neq 0$ whenever $complexvar$ is real and $complexvar>1$.
In this case, for $unitroot = e^{i angleindex}$, we have $complexvar - unitroot =
(complexvar - \cos (angleindex)) + \sin(angleindex) i$, so the real part of
$\frac{1}{complexvar-unitroot} - \frac{1}{2complexvar}$ is
\[
\frac{complexvar - \cos(angleindex)}{complexvar^2 - 2complexvar \cos(angleindex) + 1} - \frac{1}{2complexvar}
= \frac{complexvar^2-1}{2complexvar(complexvar^2 - 2complexvar \cos(angleindex) + 1)} > 0.
\]
Hence $quotientfun'(complexvar)/quotientfun(complexvar)$ has positive real part, so $quotientfun'(complexvar)/quotientfun(complexvar)$ and hence
$quotientfun(complexvar)$ are nonzero.

Applying the same argument after replacing $polynomial(complexvar)$ by $polynomial(e^{i anglevar} complexvar)$,
we deduce that $quotientfun'$ cannot have any roots outside the unit circle.
Applying the same argument after replacing $polynomial(complexvar)$ by $complexvar^{degreenum} polynomial(1/complexvar)$, we
also deduce that $quotientfun'$ cannot have any roots inside the unit circle.
Hence all roots of $quotientfun'$ have absolute value 1, as desired.

\textbf{Third solution:}
Write $polynomial(complexvar) = leadingconst \prod_{indexvar=1}^{degreenum} (complexvar - unitroot)$ and
put $unitroot = e^{2 i angleindex}$. Note that $quotientfun(e^{2 i anglevar})$
is equal to a nonzero constant times
\begin{align*}
sineprod(anglevar) &= \prod_{indexvar=1}^{degreenum} \frac{e^{i (anglevar + angleindex)}
- e^{-i(anglevar + angleindex)}}{2i} = \prod_{indexvar=1}^{degreenum} \sin(anglevar +angleindex).
\end{align*}
Since $sineprod$ has at least $2degreenum$ roots (counting multiplicity)
in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$, $sineprod'$ does also by repeated application of
Rolle's theorem. Since $quotientfun'(e^{2 i anglevar}) = 2i e^{2i anglevar} sineprod'(anglevar)$,
$quotientfun'(complexvar^2)$ has at least $2degreenum$ roots on the unit circle. Since $quotientfun'(complexvar^2)$ is equal to
$complexvar^{-degreenum-1}$ times a polynomial of degree $2degreenum$, $quotientfun'(complexvar^2)$ has all roots on the
unit circle, as then does $quotientfun'(complexvar)$.

\textbf{Remarks:}
The second solution imitates the proof of the Gauss-Lucas theorem:
the roots of the derivative of a complex polynomial lie in the convex hull of
the roots of the original polynomial.
The second solution is close to problem B3 from the 2000 Putnam.
A hybrid between the first and third solutions is to check that
on the unit circle, $\mathrm{Re}(complexvar \, quotientfun'(complexvar)/quotientfun(complexvar)) = 0$ while between any
two roots of $polynomial$, $\mathrm{Im}(complexvar \, quotientfun'(complexvar)/quotientfun(complexvar))$ runs from $+\infty$ to $-\infty$
and so must have a zero crossing. (This only works when $polynomial$ has distinct roots,
but the general case follows by the continuity of the roots of a polynomial
as functions of the coefficients.)
One can also construct a  solution using Rouch\'e's theorem.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $cloudmirror(skyladder)$ be a polynomial of degree $foxglovese$ all of whose zeros have absolute
value 1 in the complex  plane. Put $riverstone(skyladder) = cloudmirror(skyladder)/skyladder^{foxglovese/2}$. Show that all zeros
of $riverstone'(skyladder) = 0$ have absolute value 1.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Note that it is implicit in the problem that $cloudmirror$ is nonconstant,
one may take any branch of the square root, and that $skyladder=0$ should be
ignored.

\textbf{First solution:}
Write $cloudmirror(skyladder) = lanternwood \prod_{moonshaper=1}^{foxglovese} (skyladder-whisperpath)$, so that
\[
\frac{riverstone'(skyladder)}{riverstone(skyladder)} = \frac{1}{2skyladder} \sum_{moonshaper=1}^{foxglovese} \frac{skyladder+whisperpath}{skyladder-whisperpath}.
\]
Now if $skyladder\neq whisperpath$ for all $moonshaper$,then
\[
\frac{skyladder+whisperpath}{skyladder-whisperpath}
= \frac{(skyladder+whisperpath)(\overline{skyladder}-\overline{whisperpath})}{|skyladder-whisperpath|^2}
= \frac{|skyladder|^2-1+2 \mathrm{Im}(\overline{skyladder} whisperpath)}{|skyladder-whisperpath|^2},
\]
and so
\[
\mathrm{Re}\, \frac{skyladder riverstone'(skyladder)}{riverstone(skyladder)} = \frac{|skyladder|^2-1}{2} \left(
\sum_{moonshaper} \frac{1}{|skyladder-whisperpath|^2} \right).
\]
Since the quantity in parentheses
is positive, $riverstone'(skyladder)/riverstone(skyladder)$ can be $0$ only if $|skyladder|=1$. If on the other
hand $skyladder = whisperpath$ for some $moonshaper$, then $|skyladder|=1$ anyway.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Write $cloudmirror(skyladder) = lanternwood\prod_{moonshaper=1}^{foxglovese} (skyladder-whisperpath)$, so that
\[
\frac{riverstone'(skyladder)}{riverstone(skyladder)}
= \sum_{moonshaper=1}^{foxglovese} \left( \frac{1}{skyladder-whisperpath} - \frac{1}{2skyladder} \right).
\]
We first check that $riverstone'(skyladder) \neq 0$ whenever $skyladder$ is real and $skyladder>1$.
In this case, for $whisperpath = e^{i starquartzway}$, we have $skyladder - whisperpath =
(skyladder - \cos (starquartzway)) + \sin(starquartzway) i$, so the real part of
$\frac{1}{skyladder-whisperpath} - \frac{1}{2skyladder}$ is
\[
\frac{skyladder - \cos(starquartzway)}{skyladder^2 - 2skyladder \cos(starquartzway) + 1} - \frac{1}{2skyladder}
= \frac{skyladder^2-1}{2skyladder(skyladder^2 - 2skyladder \cos(starquartzway) + 1)} > 0.
\]
Hence $riverstone'(skyladder)/riverstone(skyladder)$ has positive real part, so $riverstone'(skyladder)/riverstone(skyladder)$ and hence
$riverstone(skyladder)$ are nonzero.

Applying the same argument after replacing $cloudmirror(skyladder)$ by $cloudmirror(e^{i starquartz} skyladder)$,
we deduce that $riverstone'$ cannot have any roots outside the unit circle.
Applying the same argument after replacing $cloudmirror(skyladder)$ by $skyladder^{foxglovese} cloudmirror(1/skyladder)$, we
also deduce that $riverstone'$ cannot have any roots inside the unit circle.
Hence all roots of $riverstone'$ have absolute value 1, as desired.

\textbf{Third solution:}
Write $cloudmirror(skyladder) = lanternwood \prod_{moonshaper=1}^{foxglovese} (skyladder - whisperpath)$ and
put $whisperpath = e^{2 i starquartzway}$. Note that $riverstone(e^{2 i starquartz})$
is equal to a nonzero constant times
\begin{align*}
embertrail(starquartz) &= \prod_{moonshaper=1}^{foxglovese} \frac{e^{i (starquartz + starquartzway)}
- e^{-i(starquartz + starquartzway)}}{2i} = \prod_{moonshaper=1}^{foxglovese} \sin(starquartz +starquartzway).
\end{align*}
Since $embertrail$ has at least $2foxglovese$ roots (counting multiplicity)
in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$, $embertrail'$ does also by repeated application of
Rolle's theorem. Since $riverstone'(e^{2 i starquartz}) = 2i e^{2i starquartz} embertrail'(starquartz)$,
$riverstone'(skyladder^2)$ has at least $2foxglovese$ roots on the unit circle. Since $riverstone'(skyladder^2)$ is equal to
$skyladder^{-foxglovese-1}$ times a polynomial of degree $2foxglovese$, $riverstone'(skyladder^2)$ has all roots on the
unit circle, as then does $riverstone'(skyladder)$.

\textbf{Remarks:}
The second solution imitates the proof of the Gauss-Lucas theorem:
the roots of the derivative of a complex polynomial lie in the convex hull of
the roots of the original polynomial.
The second solution is close to problem B3 from the 2000 Putnam.
A hybrid between the first and third solutions is to check that
on the unit circle, $\mathrm{Re}(skyladder riverstone'(skyladder)/riverstone(skyladder)) = 0$ while between any
two roots of $cloudmirror$, $\mathrm{Im}(skyladder riverstone'(skyladder)/riverstone(skyladder))$ runs from $+\infty$ to $-\infty$
and so must have a zero crossing. (This only works when $cloudmirror$ has distinct roots,
but the general case follows by the continuity of the roots of a polynomial
as functions of the coefficients.)
One can also construct a  solution using Rouch\'e's theorem.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $transcend(constantval)$ be a polynomial of degree $infinite$ all of whose zeros have absolute
value 1 in the complex  plane. Put $staticfunc(constantval) = transcend(constantval)/constantval^{infinite/2}$. Show that all zeros
of $staticfunc'(constantval) = 0$ have absolute value 1.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Note that it is implicit in the problem that $transcend$ is nonconstant,
one may take any branch of the square root, and that $constantval=0$ should be
ignored.

\textbf{First solution:}
Write $transcend(constantval) = variable \prod_{aggregate=1}^{infinite} (constantval-summitval)$, so that
\[
\frac{staticfunc'(constantval)}{staticfunc(constantval)} = \frac{1}{2 constantval} \sum_{aggregate=1}^{infinite} \frac{constantval+ summitval}{constantval- summitval}.
\]
Now if $constantval\neq summitval$ for all $aggregate$,then
\[
\frac{constantval+ summitval}{constantval- summitval}
= \frac{(constantval+ summitval)(\overline{constantval}-\overline{summitval})}{|constantval-summitval|^2}
= \frac{|constantval|^2-1+2 \\mathrm{Im}(\overline{constantval} summitval)}{|constantval-summitval|^2},
\]
and so
\[
\\mathrm{Re}\, \frac{constantval\, staticfunc'(constantval)}{staticfunc(constantval)} = \frac{|constantval|^2-1}{2} \left(
\sum_{aggregate} \frac{1}{|constantval-summitval|^2} \right).
\]
Since the quantity in parentheses
is positive, $staticfunc'(constantval)/staticfunc(constantval)$ can be $0$ only if $|constantval|=1$. If on the other
hand $constantval = summitval$ for some $aggregate$, then $|constantval|=1$ anyway.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Write $transcend(constantval) = variable\prod_{aggregate=1}^{infinite} (constantval-summitval)$, so that
\[
\frac{staticfunc'(constantval)}{staticfunc(constantval)}
= \sum_{aggregate=1}^{infinite} \left( \frac{1}{constantval-summitval} - \frac{1}{2 constantval} \right).
\]
We first check that $staticfunc'(constantval) \neq 0$ whenever $constantval$ is real and $constantval>1$.
In this case, for $summitval = e^{i \counteranglej}$, we have $constantval - summitval =
(constantval - \cos (\counteranglej)) + \sin(\counteranglej) i$, so the real part of
$\frac{1}{constantval-summitval} - \frac{1}{2 constantval}$ is
\[
\frac{constantval - \cos(\counteranglej)}{constantval^2 - 2 constantval \cos(\counteranglej) + 1} - \frac{1}{2 constantval}
= \frac{constantval^2-1}{2 constantval(constantval^2 - 2 constantval \cos(\counteranglej) + 1)} > 0.
\]
Hence $staticfunc'(constantval)/staticfunc(constantval)$ has positive real part, so $staticfunc'(constantval)/staticfunc(constantval)$ and hence
$staticfunc(constantval)$ are nonzero.

Applying the same argument after replacing $transcend(constantval)$ by $transcend(e^{i \counterangle} constantval)$,
we deduce that $staticfunc'$ cannot have any roots outside the unit circle.
Applying the same argument after replacing $transcend(constantval)$ by $constantval^{infinite} transcend(1/constantval)$, we
also deduce that $staticfunc'$ cannot have any roots inside the unit circle.
Hence all roots of $staticfunc'$ have absolute value $1$, as desired.

\textbf{Third solution:}
Write $transcend(constantval) = variable \prod_{aggregate=1}^{infinite} (constantval - summitval)$ and
put $summitval = e^{2 i \counteranglej}$. Note that $staticfunc(e^{2 i \counterangle})$
is equal to a nonzero constant times
\begin{align*}
monotone(\counterangle) &= \prod_{aggregate=1}^{infinite} \frac{e^{i (\counterangle + \counteranglej)}
- e^{-i(\counterangle + \counteranglej)}}{2i} = \prod_{aggregate=1}^{infinite} \sin(\counterangle +\counteranglej).
\end{align*}
Since $monotone$ has at least $2infinite$ roots (counting multiplicity)
in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$, $monotone'$ does also by repeated application of
Rolle's theorem. Since $staticfunc'(e^{2 i \counterangle}) = 2i e^{2i \counterangle} monotone'(\counterangle)$,
$staticfunc'(constantval^2)$ has at least $2infinite$ roots on the unit circle. Since $staticfunc'(constantval^2)$ is equal to
$constantval^{-infinite-1}$ times a polynomial of degree $2infinite$, $staticfunc'(constantval^2)$ has all roots on the
unit circle, as then does $staticfunc'(constantval)$.

\textbf{Remarks:}
The second solution imitates the proof of the Gauss-Lucas theorem:
the roots of the derivative of a complex polynomial lie in the convex hull of
the roots of the original polynomial.
The second solution is close to problem B3 from the 2000 Putnam.
A hybrid between the first and third solutions is to check that
on the unit circle, $\mathrm{Re}(constantval staticfunc'(constantval)/staticfunc(constantval)) = 0$ while between any
two roots of $transcend$, $\mathrm{Im}(constantval staticfunc'(constantval)/staticfunc(constantval))$ runs from $+\infty$ to $-\infty$
and so must have a zero crossing. (This only works when $transcend$ has distinct roots,
but the general case follows by the continuity of the roots of a polynomial
as functions of the coefficients.)
One can also construct a  solution using Rouch\'e's theorem.

garbled_string.question:
Let $mnbvcxqe(qzxwvtnp)$ be a polynomial of degree $poiuytrew$ all of whose zeros have absolute
value 1 in the complex  plane. Put $hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp) = mnbvcxqe(qzxwvtnp)/qzxwvtnp^{poiuytrew/2}$. Show that all zeros
of $hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp) = 0$ have absolute value 1.
garbled_string.solution:
Note that it is implicit in the problem that $mnbvcxqe$ is nonconstant,
one may take any branch of the square root, and that $qzxwvtnp=0$ should be
ignored.

\textbf{First solution:}
Write $mnbvcxqe(qzxwvtnp) = lkjhgfdsa \prod_{wsxedcrfv=1}^{poiuytrew} (qzxwvtnp-qazplmok)$, so that
\[
\frac{hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp)}{hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)} = \frac{1}{2qzxwvtnp} \sum_{wsxedcrfv=1}^{poiuytrew} \frac{qzxwvtnp+qazplmok}{qzxwvtnp-qazplmok}.
\]
Now if $qzxwvtnp\neq qazplmok$ for all $wsxedcrfv$, then
\[
\frac{qzxwvtnp+qazplmok}{qzxwvtnp-qazplmok}
= \frac{(qzxwvtnp+qazplmok)(\overline{qzxwvtnp}-\overline{qazplmok})}{|qzxwvtnp-qazplmok|^2}
= \frac{|qzxwvtnp|^2-1+2 \\mathrm{Im}(\overline{qzxwvtnp} \, qazplmok)}{|qzxwvtnp-qazplmok|^2},
\]
and so
\[
\\mathrm{Re}\, \frac{qzxwvtnp \, hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp)}{hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)} = \frac{|qzxwvtnp|^2-1}{2} \left(
\sum_{wsxedcrfv} \frac{1}{|qzxwvtnp-qazplmok|^2} \right).
\]
Since the quantity in parentheses
is positive, $hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp)/hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)$ can be $0$ only if $|qzxwvtnp|=1$. If on the other
hand $qzxwvtnp = qazplmok$ for some $wsxedcrfv$, then $|qzxwvtnp|=1$ anyway.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Write $mnbvcxqe(qzxwvtnp) = lkjhgfdsa\prod_{wsxedcrfv=1}^{poiuytrew} (qzxwvtnp-qazplmok)$, so that
\[
\frac{hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp)}{hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)}
= \sum_{wsxedcrfv=1}^{poiuytrew} \left( \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp-qazplmok} - \frac{1}{2qzxwvtnp} \right).
\]
We first check that $hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp) \neq 0$ whenever $qzxwvtnp$ is real and $qzxwvtnp>1$.
In this case, for $qazplmok = e^{i \xswedcvfr}$, we have $qzxwvtnp - qazplmok =
(qzxwvtnp - \cos (\xswedcvfr)) + \sin(\xswedcvfr) i$, so the real part of
$\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp-qazplmok} - \frac{1}{2qzxwvtnp}$ is
\[
\frac{qzxwvtnp - \cos(\xswedcvfr)}{qzxwvtnp^2 - 2qzxwvtnp \cos(\xswedcvfr) + 1} - \frac{1}{2qzxwvtnp}
= \frac{qzxwvtnp^2-1}{2qzxwvtnp(qzxwvtnp^2 - 2qzxwvtnp \cos(\xswedcvfr) + 1)} > 0.
\]
Hence $hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp)/hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)$ has positive real part, so $hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp)/hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)$ and hence
$hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)$ are nonzero.

Applying the same argument after replacing $mnbvcxqe(qzxwvtnp)$ by $mnbvcxqe(e^{i \klmnbvcz} qzxwvtnp)$,
we deduce that $hjgrksla'$ cannot have any roots outside the unit circle.
Applying the same argument after replacing $mnbvcxqe(qzxwvtnp)$ by $qzxwvtnp^{poiuytrew} mnbvcxqe(1/qzxwvtnp)$, we
also deduce that $hjgrksla'$ cannot have any roots inside the unit circle.
Hence all roots of $hjgrksla'$ have absolute value 1, as desired.

\textbf{Third solution:}
Write $mnbvcxqe(qzxwvtnp) = lkjhgfdsa \prod_{wsxedcrfv=1}^{poiuytrew} (qzxwvtnp - qazplmok)$ and
put $qazplmok = e^{2 i \xswedcvfr}$. Note that $hjgrksla(e^{2 i \klmnbvcz})$
is equal to a nonzero constant times
\begin{align*}
trsdifgh(\klmnbvcz) &= \prod_{wsxedcrfv=1}^{poiuytrew} \frac{e^{i (\klmnbvcz + \xswedcvfr)}
- e^{-i(\klmnbvcz + \xswedcvfr)}}{2i} = \prod_{wsxedcrfv=1}^{poiuytrew} \sin(\klmnbvcz +\xswedcvfr).
\end{align*}
Since $trsdifgh$ has at least $2poiuytrew$ roots (counting multiplicity)
in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$, $trsdifgh'$ does also by repeated application of
Rolle's theorem. Since $hjgrksla'(e^{2 i \klmnbvcz}) = 2i e^{2i \klmnbvcz} trsdifgh'(\klmnbvcz)$,
$hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp^2)$ has at least $2poiuytrew$ roots on the unit circle. Since $hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp^2)$ is equal to
$qzxwvtnp^{-poiuytrew-1}$ times a polynomial of degree $2poiuytrew$, $hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp^2)$ has all roots on the
unit circle, as then does $hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp)$.

\textbf{Remarks:}
The second solution imitates the proof of the Gauss-Lucas theorem:
the roots of the derivative of a complex polynomial lie in the convex hull of
the roots of the original polynomial.
The second solution is close to problem B3 from the 2000 Putnam.
A hybrid between the first and third solutions is to check that
on the unit circle, $\\mathrm{Re}(qzxwvtnp hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp)/hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)) = 0$ while between any
two roots of $mnbvcxqe$, $\\mathrm{Im}(qzxwvtnp hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp)/hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp))$ runs from $+\\infty$ to $-\\infty$
and so must have a zero crossing. (This only works when $mnbvcxqe$ has distinct roots,
but the general case follows by the continuity of the roots of a polynomial
as functions of the coefficients.)
One can also construct a  solution using Rouch\'e's theorem.

kernel_variant.question:
Fix the radius R = 3.  Let f(z) be a non-constant polynomial of degree k \geq  1 whose zeros s_1,\ldots ,s_k all satisfy |s_j| = R.  Choose a ray L emanating from the origin - for definiteness the negative real axis (-\infty ,0] - and delete it from the plane:
  \Omega  := \mathbb{C} \ L.
On the simply-connected domain \Omega  fix the principal branch Log z of the logarithm and, for every z \in  \Omega , put
  z^{k/2} := exp((k/2)\cdot Log z).
(When k is odd, this amounts to fixing a single-valued branch of the square root.)  Define the holomorphic function
  h(z) := f(z) / z^{k/2}	 (z \in  \Omega , z \neq  0).
Prove that every zero of the derivative h'(z) lies on the circle |z| = R.
kernel_variant.solution:
Factor f :
  f(z) = a \prod _{j = 1}^{k} (z - s_j),  |s_j| = R = 3, a \in  \mathbb{C}\{0}.
Because h(z) = f(z) / z^{k/2} on \Omega , logarithmic differentiation gives
  h'(z)/h(z) = f'(z)/f(z) - k/(2z)
        = \Sigma _{j = 1}^{k} ( 1/(z - s_j) - 1/(2z) )
        = (1/2z) \Sigma _{j = 1}^{k} (z + s_j)/(z - s_j).
Hence
(1)	 z\cdot h'(z)/h(z) = (1/2) \Sigma _{j = 1}^{k} (z + s_j)/(z - s_j).

A geometric identity.
For any fixed zero s with |s| = R and any z \in  \Omega  \ {s}
  Re [ (z + s)/(z - s) ]
  = Re [ (z + s)(\overline z - \overline s) / |z - s|^2 ]
  = (|z|^2 - |s|^2) / |z - s|^2
  = (|z|^2 - R^2) / |z - s|^2.

Taking real parts in (1) therefore yields
  Re [ z\cdot h'(z)/h(z) ]
  = (|z|^2 - R^2)/2 \cdot  \Sigma _{j = 1}^{k} 1/|z - s_j|^2.   (2)

The sum in (2) is strictly positive whenever z \neq  s_j for every j.  Consequently
  Re [ z\cdot h'(z)/h(z) ] = 0 \Rightarrow  |z| = R.                               (3)

Locating the zeros of h'.
Let z_0 \in  \Omega  satisfy h'(z_0) = 0.

* If z_0 \neq  s_j for all j, then h'(z_0) = 0 implies h'(z_0)/h(z_0) = 0, hence z_0\cdot h'(z_0)/h(z_0) = 0.  By (3) we obtain |z_0| = R.

* If z_0 = s_m for some m, then by the hypothesis on the zeros of f we again have |z_0| = R.

Thus every zero of h' lies on the circle |z| = R, completing the proof.

Branch considerations.
All operations (division by z, logarithmic differentiation, taking real parts) are carried out in the domain \Omega  on which the chosen branch z^{k/2} is single-valued and holomorphic.  The argument above therefore applies equally whether k is even or odd.

core_steps: Express p(z)=c·∏(z−r_j) with |r_j|=1 and write the logarithmic derivative: g'(z)/g(z)= (1/2z)∑(z+r_j)/(z−r_j).
Multiply by z and take real parts: Re[z·g'(z)/g(z)] = (|z|^2−1)/2 · ∑ 1/|z−r_j|^2.
Note the summation factor is positive unless z coincides with a root.
Hence Re[z·g'(z)/g(z)] vanishes only when |z|=1 (or when z=r_j, which already has |z|=1).
Conclude that every zero of g'(z) lies on the unit circle.

slot1.description: Radius of the circle on which all roots r_j lie (scaling of the problem)

slot2.description: Symbol n denoting the (positive) degree/number of roots of p

slot3.description: Leading constant c in the factorization p(z)=c∏(z−r_j)
